summary: McKenzie weighs the pros and cons of choosing Java for his micro-ISV (uISV) desktop application, Bingo Card Creator. He systematically addresses common objections—JRE installation requirements, speed concerns, and ugly Swing UIs—and argues each is manageable with practical workarounds. He notes that JRE penetration is higher than critics assume, Launch4j can wrap JARs in native Windows executables, and Java's speed is adequate for his use case. He also highlights Java's cross-platform portability and free development tools as decisive advantages over Visual Studio .NET, which he can't legally use at home.
key_points: JRE installation is less of a barrier than commonly claimed—95% of tracked users had some Java version, and tools like Launch4j can guide users through installation
Java's perceived slowness is irrelevant for his application; startup delay is masked with splash screens that double as upgrade prompts
Cross-platform portability is a genuine advantage, especially for targeting both Mac and Windows from a single codebase
Free tooling (Eclipse, NetBeans) gives Java a decisive edge over Visual Studio .NET for a bootstrapped solo developer who needs legal licenses at home
Practical engineering tradeoffs matter more than theoretical language debates—every objection to Java has a workable mitigation
money_quotes: Getting accurate statistics on Internet users is like herding cats.
It weighs in at 18 MB, which is more than a coffee break.
Java is just not slow at what I am doing if you are using a computer which is anything close to recent.
I display another splash screen for 5 seconds offering them the opportunity to upgrade to the full version before dumping them into the main window.
I'm just not enough of an expert with the Windows APIs to do a GUI in a reasonable amount of time without the .NET safety-net under me.
key_insight: Technology choices for a micro-ISV should be driven by practical constraints like licensing costs, developer familiarity, and cross-platform needs rather than by conventional wisdom about a language's theoretical shortcomings.
